title: Myositis ossificans (heterotopic bone)
vignette: A 20-year-old man comes to the office due to pain in the left thigh. Three months ago, a horse kicked him in the left midthigh, resulting in deep pain and swelling in the thigh muscles; x-ray was normal, and, over the next several days, the symptoms resolved with rest and ice packs. However, a few weeks later, the patient gradually developed recurrent pain and swelling in the same area. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows a solitary, hard, mobile, oval mass of approximately 4 x 5 cm in the anterior aspect of the left thigh. Excision of the mass is performed. Histologic examination is likely to show which of the following?

clues.text: a horse kicked him in the left midthigh
clues.note: A blunt-force muscle contusion is the classic trigger for myositis ossificans. Severe or repeated muscle trauma sets off heterotopic bone formation.

clues.text: deep pain and swelling in the thigh muscles
clues.note: The injury is INTRAMUSCULAR (deep, within the muscle belly). Heterotopic ossification develops within the injured muscle, not in a joint or tendon sheath.
An initially normal x-ray fits a fresh contusion with no lesion yet. Ossification takes weeks to appear, which is why the early film is clean.

clues.text: a few weeks later, the patient gradually developed recurrent pain and swelling
clues.note: The telltale time course: injury seems to resolve, then a mass and pain RECUR over weeks in the same muscle. This delayed-recurrence pattern is the signature of myositis ossificans.
A HARD (ossified) yet MOBILE mass within muscle fits heterotopic bone, not a soft cyst or a malignant tumor fixed to bone. The firmness comes from metaplastic bone.

whatMatters: The real ask is 'what will the EXCISED mass look like under the microscope.' First convert the story — blunt muscle trauma, then a hard, mobile, recurring intramuscular mass over weeks — into the diagnosis: myositis ossificans (heterotopic bone in soft tissue). Its histology is benign metaplastic bone with surrounding fibroblastic proliferation (no atypia). Answer A.
strategy: When a hard mass appears WEEKS after muscle trauma in a young patient, think myositis ossificans and expect a benign 'zonal' pattern (mature bone rim, fibroblastic/immature center, no mitotic atypia). Its danger is being mistaken for osteosarcoma — but the bone here is benign and metaplastic, and a true sarcoma wouldn't appear within 2 months on a previously normal x-ray.

options.text: Benign metaplastic bone with surrounding fibroblastic proliferation
Closely packed, small, round, uniform neoplastic cells
Mature adipocytes with focal large, atypical hyperchromatic cells
Pleomorphic neoplastic spindle cells producing new woven bone
Synovial cyst filled with gelatinous mucoid material

eliminations.label: small round blue cells = Ewing sarcoma
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: closely packed small round uniform cells are the histology of Ewing sarcoma (t(11;22)), which causes bone pain/swelling, sometimes with fever, in young patients. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that's a malignant primary bone tumor fixed to bone — not a mobile, post-traumatic muscle mass that came up over weeks. ✅ Correct if: a destructive diaphyseal bone lesion with onion-skin periosteal reaction.

eliminations.label: atypical cells in fat = liposarcoma
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: mature adipocytes with large, atypical, hyperchromatic cells (lipoblasts) describe liposarcoma. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: liposarcoma is rare, usually age >50, and grows as a slow, painless deep mass — not a hard mass appearing weeks after a kick. ✅ Correct if: an older adult with a deep, enlarging, painless fatty mass.

eliminations.label: malignant cells making osteoid = osteosarcoma
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: pleomorphic spindle cells producing woven bone/osteoid are osteosarcoma. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: osteosarcoma arises in the metaphysis (e.g., distal femur), is fixed to bone, and can't appear within ~2 months on a previously normal x-ray; myositis-ossificans bone is BENIGN and metaplastic (no atypia). ✅ Correct if: Codman triangle/sunburst pattern, metaphyseal mass with malignant osteoid.

eliminations.label: gelatinous cyst = ganglion/synovial cyst (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a synovial/ganglion cyst is an outpouching of joint capsule or tendon sheath filled with clear, gelatinous mucoid fluid — soft and rubbery. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: those occur near joints (dorsal wrist, foot), are SOFT and cystic, and don't follow muscle trauma; this is a HARD mass in the mid-thigh muscle. ✅ Correct if: a soft, transilluminating nodule over the dorsal wrist.

reasoning: Blunt muscle trauma -> initial injury resolves, then a hard mass recurs over weeks in the same muscle
A hard, mobile, intramuscular mass weeks after trauma = myositis ossificans (heterotopic bone)
Histology shows a zonal pattern: benign metaplastic bone with surrounding fibroblastic proliferation, no malignant atypia
mechanism: Myositis ossificans is heterotopic (extraskeletal) ossification triggered by trauma — typically muscle contusion or fracture — that induces expression of bone morphogenetic proteins. Spindle-shaped stem cells migrate in and differentiate into fibroblasts, then chondrocytes and osteoblasts, producing a characteristic ZONAL pattern: a more mature, ossified outer rim around an inner zone of fibroblastic proliferation and immature tissue, all WITHOUT mitotic atypia. It presents weeks after injury as a painful, firm, mobile mass, classically in the quadriceps. The benign metaplastic bone distinguishes it from osteosarcoma, which it can mimic radiographically.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid MSK/Path > myositis ossificans: post-traumatic heterotopic ossification, benign zonal metaplastic bone, radiographically mimics but is NOT osteosarcoma. Contrast Ewing, osteosarcoma, liposarcoma, ganglion cyst.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: (1) diagnose myositis ossificans from the clinical story (trauma -> hard, mobile, recurring muscle mass), then (2) pick its histology. Don't answer straight from the word 'mass.'

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on heterotopic/metaplastic ossification, zonal pattern, woven bone, fibroblastic proliferation, ganglion cyst, lipoblast? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Synovial/ganglion cyst (E) is the trap if you anchor on 'mass' without the hard, post-traumatic, intramuscular details — cysts are soft, juxta-articular, and gelatinous, not hard ossified muscle.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: trauma + hard, mobile muscle mass appearing over weeks = myositis ossificans; benign metaplastic bone, NOT osteosarcoma.

keywords.front: Trauma -> hard, mobile muscle mass weeks later

keywords.back: Zonal benign metaplastic bone + fibroblastic proliferation (no atypia)

keywords.front: Small round blue cells, bone, t(11;22)
Pleomorphic spindle cells making osteoid, metaphysis
Soft gelatinous cyst at dorsal wrist

firstAid.detail: MSK Path > heterotopic ossification after trauma; benign zonal metaplastic bone; radiographically mimics but is NOT osteosarcoma.
firstAid.location: MSK Path > heterotopic ossification after trauma; benign zonal metaplastic bone; radiographically mimics but is NOT osteosarcoma.

firstAid.detail: MSK Path > Ewing (small round blue, t(11;22), onion-skin), osteosarcoma (metaphysis, Codman/sunburst), liposarcoma (older, painless), ganglion cyst.
firstAid.location: MSK Path > Ewing (small round blue, t(11;22), onion-skin), osteosarcoma (metaphysis, Codman/sunburst), liposarcoma (older, painless), ganglion cyst.

firstAid.detail: Embryo/Path > endochondral ossification and BMP signaling driving osteoblast differentiation.
firstAid.location: Embryo/Path > endochondral ossification and BMP signaling driving osteoblast differentiation.

memoryHook: Bruised muscle that turns to BONE = myositis ossificans — benign metaplastic bone, NOT cancer.
